mnemonics: SBLA: Socket → Bind → Listen → Accept (TCP server sequence)
CSN: Connect → Send → Notify (Client sequence for TCP)
HIBF: htons(), inet_pton(), bind(), fork() - common socket operations
BEAN: Bind, Establish, Accept, Nurture (Server startup)

acronyms.usage: Connection-oriented, reliable, ordered delivery. Used for HTTP, FTP, SSH.
Connectionless, unreliable but fast. Used for DNS, streaming, VoIP.
Berkeley sockets API, the standard Unix socket interface.
Communication between processes, includes sockets, pipes, message queues.
Indicates no more data to read; in sockets, detected by recv() returning 0.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a phone call: Client dials (SYN) → Server answers (SYN-ACK) → Client confirms (ACK). Only then conversation (data transfer) begins.
Think of a socket as a telephone. socket() buys a phone, bind() gives it a number, listen() turns ringer on, accept() picks up the call, send/recv() are the conversation.
Picture a restaurant with limited tables. The backlog is like the waiting area. When full, new customers (connections) are turned away.
Lifecycle: Created (socket) → Bound (bind) → Listening (listen) → Connected (accept/connect) → Data exchange (send/recv) → Closed (close)

analogies.analogy: TCP is like a phone call - you dial, wait for connection, talk with guaranteed delivery. UDP is like sending postcards - no connection needed, may get lost, but faster.
The server socket is like a switchboard operator (listening for calls). The connected socket is the actual phone call between two people.
IP address is like a building address, port number is like a specific office/room in that building. Port 80 in HTTP is like the reception desk.
Like a restaurant where each customer gets their own dedicated waiter (child process) while the host (parent) continues greeting new customers.
